summary: Gruber ran a poll across Mastodon, Threads, and Twitter/X asking FineWoven iPhone case owners about durability, and the results were damning: 53% of 7,059 respondents called them 'a piece of junk.' He documents specific failure modes — the FineWoven material wearing poorly and plastic sides delaminating — while noting some early production issues may have been fixed. Gruber concludes that Apple needs to go back to the drawing board, as FineWoven lacks leather's ability to age gracefully. He also uses the poll as a lens to observe shifting social media dynamics, noting Mastodon's declining engagement while Threads appears to be thriving.
key_points: Over half of 7,059 poll respondents rated Apple's FineWoven cases as 'a piece of junk' after just five months of use, with only 21% saying they held up well
FineWoven failures come in two forms: the material itself wearing poorly and the plastic sides delaminating, the latter possibly a fixable early-production issue
A $60 Apple case that looks like crap after a few months fails the premium product standard that Apple case buyers expect
Mastodon responses were significantly more favorable toward FineWoven, which Gruber theorizes reflects a more careful, boutique user base
Social media engagement shifted dramatically: Mastodon dropped from leading Twitter in April 2023 polls to a distant third, while Threads emerged as a strong second platform
money_quotes: I wouldn't call these results disastrous, but they're pretty bad, with over half of participants choosing 'It's a piece of junk', and only one-fifth choosing 'It's held up well.'
So by Apple's standards, maybe this does qualify as disastrous.
Good leather products often look good as they age and get used; FineWoven doesn't have that quality.
Apple's not going back to leather, but they need a better-than-leather replacement, and FineWoven 1.0 isn't it.

People buy Apple cases (and wallets, and watch bands) because they expect them to be great products. A $60 iPhone case (or $60 MagSafe Wallet) that looks like crap after just a few months of use is not a great product.
I sort of spitefully ignored the breakage, hoping Twitter/X would fade into irrelevance. But that seemingly isn't happening.
So I'm thinking maybe the sort of people who prefer Mastodon over Threads or Twitter are the sort of people who are a bit more likely to treat their devices — even in cases — carefully.
key_insight: Apple's FineWoven cases fail the basic premium product test — a $60 case that degrades visibly in months is unacceptable by Apple's own standards, and the material needs a complete rethink.
